scenario.title: AI Productivity Tracking at Warehouse
scenario.proposal: Deploy AI-powered cameras, wearables, and algorithmic performance scoring for all warehouse workers
scenario.context: A fulfillment center with ~500 workers, mostly immigrant and working-class. Three workers have already been fired based on algorithmic recommendations. No union.
scenario.stakes: Job security, worker dignity, surveillance normalization, algorithmic accountability, language access

members.name: Nadia
members.role: Shift Supervisor — promoted from floor, caught between workers and management

members.name: Graciela
members.role: Night Shift Worker — single parent, can't afford to lose this job

members.name: Carmen
members.role: Safety Inspector — concerned about wearable device health effects

members.name: Oleg
members.role: HR Representative — implements policies, uncomfortable with AI firings

members.name: Emmanuel
members.role: Warehouse Veteran — 20 years, seen every management fad come and go

members.name: Keiko
members.role: New Hire — 3 months in, already anxious about the tracking

members.name: Bao
members.role: Forklift Operator — skilled role, worried about being scored like pickers

members.name: Oscar
members.role: Union Organizer (external) — trying to help workers organize

statements.speaker: Nadia
statements.text: I just want to say right off the bat, introducing AI-powered cameras and wearables feels like a massive invasion of our privacy. We've already seen three people lose their jobs based on some algorithm's recommendation, and that's just unacceptable. Our workers need job security, not constant surveillance. And to be honest, the workers in our facility deserve to be treated with respect, not as data points to be analyzed. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Graciela
statements.text: I completely agree with you, Nadia. As a night shift worker and a single parent, job security is everything to me. Losing even one shift could mean not being able to provide for my child. If we start introducing these AI systems, it feels like we're just one step away from more layoffs, and I can't risk that for my family. We deserve to work in an environment that respects us, not surveils us. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Carmen
statements.text: I completely understand the concerns around job security and privacy, and I agree that workers deserve respect and transparency. However, I believe that if we approach this AI deployment thoughtfully, particularly with wearable devices, there can be significant benefits. By using wearables, we can actually enhance worker safety and health monitoring, addressing fatigue or potential workplace hazards before they escalate. These devices can provide real-time data that could protect our workers instead of surveilling them. That said, I do think we need stringent guidelines to ensure data is used ethically and transparently to build trust. (stance: support)

statements.speaker: Oleg
statements.text: I appreciate what Nadia and Graciela have expressed; their concerns about job security and privacy resonate deeply with me. I also want to stress that while Carmen has highlighted potential safety benefits, any implementation of AI, especially systems that could lead to firings or constant monitoring, should come with clear, strict guidelines and genuine communication with our workers. We need to ensure that any technology we introduce genuinely supports our employees rather than undermining their livelihoods and dignity. If we can't guarantee that, then I’m not comfortable proceeding. (stance: undecided)

statements.speaker: Emmanuel
statements.text: I hear you, Nadia and Graciela, and I share those concerns. After two decades in the warehouse, I have seen these management fads come and go, often leaving workers feeling like cogs in a machine. Carmen, I understand the idea of wearables for safety benefits, but I worry that they’ll just pile on the pressure to perform and keep us under a microscope. What’s next? A score for how well we’re doing our jobs? We need to keep our jobs and dignity intact above all else. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Keiko
statements.text: I completely share the concerns raised by Nadia, Graciela, and Emmanuel about job security and privacy. The idea of introducing AI-powered devices feels overly intrusive, especially for someone who’s still learning the ropes here. Instead of feeling empowered, I worry these systems will create more pressure and anxiety, adding to the already challenging environment. We need to be focusing on supporting each other, not being tracked like we're constantly under scrutiny. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Bao
statements.text: I hear all your concerns loud and clear. Like you, I’m really worried that adding AI systems, especially those that could track our performance or push us to work faster, will just lead to more stress. I don’t want to be treated like a number or have my job depend on some algorithm. But I do see Carmen's point about potential safety benefits. If wearables can actually help prevent injuries and not just spy on us, that could be something worth considering. Still, I want to make sure any implementation keeps us informed and not under constant pressure. Overall, I’m leaning towards conditional support if we can ensure clear guidelines and worker protections. (stance: conditional)

statements.speaker: Oscar
statements.text: I really hear all the concerns about privacy and job security that have been raised. It's crucial we prioritize our dignity and well-being at work. However, I believe we can harness AI thoughtfully and strategically. If we use these tools to genuinely enhance safety and health without the added pressure of surveillance, it could lead to a more supportive environment. We just need to make sure we’re involved in creating the guidelines and that our voices are heard in the process — that way, we create a system that works for us, not against us. (stance: support)

statements.speaker: Nadia
statements.text: I just want to emphasize again how concerning it is that we're even considering AI-powered cameras and performance scoring in our workplace. It feels like we're stepping into a dangerous territory where our jobs and our dignity are on the line. I understand the arguments for safety benefits, but based on my experience, the reality is these systems could become tools to further micromanage us and increase stress, not support us. Without proper protections and clear guidelines, I firmly believe we should oppose this proposal. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Carmen
statements.text: Nadia, I hear your concerns, and I completely respect your perspective on the potential misuse of these systems. However, I still believe there’s a way to implement AI-powered wearables that genuinely prioritize worker safety rather than micromanagement. With proper safeguards in place, such as strong privacy protections and guidelines on data usage, we can help prevent accidents and health issues, especially in physically demanding roles. I think it’s essential we focus on making these tools work for us and not against us. We should work together to shape the implementation so it truly serves our needs without compromising our dignity. (stance: support)

statements.speaker: Oscar
statements.text: I appreciate Nadia for highlighting the risks of micromanagement and job insecurity. It's a real concern that resonates with many of us. But Carmen's point about using AI-powered wearables to enhance safety and prevent injuries is important too. If we can establish strong guidelines and protections that prioritize our well-being, perhaps this technology could work in our favor. But I want to stress that we cannot afford to compromise our dignity in the process; we must be at the forefront of shaping these guidelines. (stance: support)

statements.speaker: Graciela
statements.text: I hear both Nadia and Carmen's points, but I have to stick to my original concerns. While I appreciate the idea of using AI for safety, the reality is that there's a huge risk of these systems being misused. As a night shift worker dealing with constant pressure, I'm worried that instead of helping us, these devices will just lead to more micromanagement and stress. We can't put our livelihoods on the line with hopeful "safeguards" that might not protect us when it counts. If we can't trust that our jobs won’t be jeopardized, I believe it’s better to oppose this proposal. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Keiko
statements.text: I completely understand Nadia and Graciela's concerns about the potential for misuse and micromanagement. It's vital to recognize that no matter how "safeguards" are proposed, they often leave us vulnerable to being treated like we’re constantly under a microscope. While Carmen and Oscar make valid points about potential safety benefits, the reality is that any system that could compromise our privacy and job security feels too risky to me. The stakes are too high to move forward without absolute certainty that our dignity won't be sacrificed. We need to prioritize our well-being over any possible technological gain. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Emmanuel
statements.text: I hear all of you loud and clear, especially Nadia, Graciela, and Keiko. The worry that these AI systems will end up being tools for micromanagement is completely valid. I’ve been around long enough to see how management loves to use new technology as a way to keep a tighter grip on workers instead of really supporting us. Carmen and Oscar, I appreciate your optimism about potential safety improvements, but history tells me that these systems often come with hidden downsides that outweigh the benefits. We’ve gotta protect our jobs and dignity above all else. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Oleg
statements.text: I appreciate everyone's contributions here. Nadia and Graciela have raised critical points that resonate with my own fears about the potential for micromanagement and job insecurity. While Carmen and Oscar emphasize the safety benefits of AI, I worry that those advantages might not outweigh the risks we face—especially as Emmanuel pointed out from his experience. If we can't establish robust protections to ensure our privacy and job security, it does feel too risky to consider this proposal. Therefore, I'm leaning towards a strong opposition unless we can guarantee those fundamental protections. (stance: oppose)

statements.speaker: Bao
statements.text: I appreciate everyone’s insights, especially Nadia and Graciela, for highlighting the very real concerns about being micromanaged and the risks to our jobs. I understand Carmen and Oscar's optimism about safety benefits, but the truth is I’ve seen how easily “safeguards” can turn into just another way for management to keep tabs on us. Without clear, enforceable protections in place, I lean towards opposition as well. We cannot allow our dignity and job security to hang in the balance while they experiment with these technologies. (stance: oppose)

analysis:
The community deliberation surrounding the proposal to deploy AI-powered cameras and wearables in the warehouse was marked by a robust debate driven primarily by concerns over job security and privacy. Nadia emerged as a vocal opponent of the proposal, emphasizing the invasion of privacy and its threat to job stability, stating, “Our workers need job security, not constant surveillance.” This sentiment was strongly echoed by Graciela, Emmanuel, and Keiko, who collectively influenced others against the AI systems, portraying them as potential tools for micromanagement rather than support.

Coalitions formed predominantly among the opposition, with a core group of workers sharing identical concerns about the ramifications of AI on their lives, largely consisting of Nadia, Graciela, Emmanuel, and Keiko. Carmen and Oscar represented the support coalition, arguing for the benefits of enhanced safety through thoughtful use of technology. Carmen's assertion that there could be “significant benefits” from AI, if implemented ethically, highlighted a crucial turning point as it spurred a brief moment of conditional support from Bao, who expressed cautious openness to safety improvements.

Ultimately, the outcome reaffirmed the dominance of the opposition coalition, with six workers, including Oleg and Bao, voting against the proposal. Their widespread concern about potential misuse of technology ultimately overshadowed the support for AI benefits.

Notably absent from the discussion were voices advocating for outright management perspectives or technological experts who could have articulated the potential advantages more vividly. This absence limited a fuller exploration of the balance between safety and job dignity.

The power dynamics revealed that established workers with firsthand experience of managerial practices held significant sway in the discussion, thus underscoring their collective influence in shaping the community’s stance against the implementation of potentially intrusive technologies.
